title: React Solution Architect Assessment
description: A high-stakes evaluation for Architect-level roles. Covers distributed frontend systems, core engine internals, design system governance, and enterprise-grade performance strategies.

questions.text: In a Federated Micro-Frontend (MFE) architecture, how should global state (e.g., Auth) be shared while maintaining team autonomy?
options: Shared Redux store with dynamic injection of reducers
Event-driven communication via a custom Event Bus or Window Dispatch
questions.explanation: Event-driven architecture decouples teams. Redux stores create tight coupling and versioning conflicts between MFEs.

questions.text: Which React 18 feature allows for the prioritization of 'Input' events over 'Data-driven' renders to improve INP (Interaction to Next Paint)?
questions.explanation: Lanes (bitmask-based priorities) allow React to interrupt low-priority rendering for discrete user input.

questions.text: What is the primary risk of using 'Module Federation' for shared component libraries in a production environment?
options: Version mismatch and breaking changes at runtime (Lack of compile-time safety)
questions.explanation: Since MFEs are deployed independently, a shared 'remotes' library update can break consumers without build-time warnings.

questions.text: How does 'Selective Hydration' handle a user click on a non-hydrated component while the rest of the tree is still processing?
options: It ignores the click until full hydration is complete
It prioritizes the clicked component's hydration and replays the event
It forces a full page reload
questions.explanation: React 18's Selective Hydration uses 'Event Replay' to capture and re-fire clicks after prioritizing that specific chunk's hydration.

questions.text: When building an 'Atomic Design' system, which layer should strictly hold business logic according to architectural best practices?
questions.explanation: To maximize reuse, Atoms/Molecules/Organisms should be 'dumb' components; logic resides in Pages or Template-level containers.

questions.text: What is the 'Double Buffer' strategy in the React Fiber reconciler?
options: A way to render two versions of a component simultaneously for A/B testing
The use of 'current' and 'workInProgress' trees to manage non-blocking UI updates
Storing state in both memory and IndexedDB
A method for handling high-frequency window resizing
questions.explanation: Double buffering allows React to commit a complete tree only after calculating all changes in an off-screen 'workInProgress' tree.

questions.text: In a large-scale application, why might an architect choose 'Zustand' over 'Redux Toolkit' for local-global state?
options: Redux is inherently slower than Zustand
Zustand has a smaller footprint and significantly less boilerplate for mid-sized complexity
Zustand does not use the Context API
questions.explanation: Zustand provides a simpler API surface while still avoiding the 're-render all' pitfalls of standard Context.

questions.text: What is the 'Islands Architecture' primary advantage over standard SPA Hydration?
options: Zero JavaScript for static parts, reducing the TBT (Total Blocking Time)
questions.explanation: It avoids 'Top-Down' hydration, which blocks the main thread, by only sending JS for the interactive 'islands'.

questions.text: How do 'React Server Components' (RSC) solve the 'Waterfall' problem in data fetching?
options: By moving fetching to the server, where latency to the database is minimal
By automatically batching all client-side requests
By using WebSockets for all communication
questions.explanation: Since RSCs run on the server, they can fetch data and render to stream in a single RTT (Round Trip Time) to the client.

questions.text: Which hook is required to prevent 'tearing' in concurrent mode when subscribing to an external non-React store?
questions.explanation: This hook forces a synchronous update when the store changes, maintaining consistency during time-slicing.

questions.text: What is the role of a 'Boundary component' (Error Boundary or Suspense) in an architectural sense?
options: To provide fault tolerance and declarative orchestration of async states
questions.explanation: Boundaries allow the system to remain partially interactive even when certain sub-modules fail or are loading.

questions.text: In the context of 'TBT' (Total Blocking Time), what is the impact of excessive 'React.memo' usage?
options: It can increase TBT if the overhead of prop comparison exceeds the cost of re-rendering
It has no impact on TBT
questions.explanation: Memoization is a CPU-memory trade-off. Shallow comparing huge prop objects on every render can be more expensive than the render itself.

questions.text: What is 'Partial Hydration'?
options: Hydrating only certain interactive components while leaving the rest as static HTML
Using only 50% of the CPU for hydration
questions.explanation: Partial hydration targets specific nodes, which is the core of architectures like Astro.

questions.text: Why does React 18 use 'Bitmasks' for Lanes?
options: To allow efficient checking of multiple priority levels in a single operation
Because JavaScript only supports bitwise math
questions.explanation: Bitmasks allow the scheduler to determine if a lane is 'cluttered' or needs to be processed with extreme speed.

questions.text: What is the primary benefit of 'Streaming SSR'?
options: Faster FP (First Paint) and LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) as the browser can render HTML chunks as they arrive
questions.explanation: Streaming allows the browser to progressively render the UI without waiting for the entire server-side string to be generated.

questions.text: What is 'Referential Identity' and why is it critical for architects to enforce its management?
options: Ensuring objects/functions don't change pointers on re-render, preventing unnecessary downstream re-renders
questions.explanation: Unstable identities (e.g., inlining functions) break optimizations like React.memo and useEffect dependencies.

questions.text: How should an architect handle 'Transitive Dependencies' in a monorepo setup?
options: Declare every dependency in the root package.json
Use 'Peer Dependencies' to ensure only one version of a library like React exists in the bundle
Ignore them; the bundler will handle it
questions.explanation: React requires a single instance. Duplicates (multiple versions) cause hook errors and context mismatches.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the `useInsertionEffect` hook?
options: To insert elements into the DOM during the commit phase
To inject dynamic styles for CSS-in-JS libraries before layout is calculated
questions.explanation: It fires before useLayoutEffect and is exclusively for library authors to inject <style> tags safely.

questions.text: What is a 'Pure Component' function in the context of React internals?
options: A function that is idempotent and has no side effects during the render phase
A component with only HTML tags
A component that cannot be updated
questions.explanation: Purity allows React to safely restart or abort renders in concurrent mode.

questions.text: In 'Render-as-you-fetch', where is the data request typically initiated?
options: In the useEffect of the component
Before the component even renders (e.g., on route change or event)
questions.explanation: Starting the fetch 'early' avoids the waterfall where the component must mount before it knows what to fetch.

questions.text: Which metric is the best indicator of the success of a 'React Virtualization' implementation?
questions.explanation: Virtualization keeps the DOM node count constant regardless of list size, preventing memory and layout overhead.

questions.text: What is the primary difference between 'Bailout' and 'Eager State Updates' in React?
options: Bailout skips the render; Eager updates apply the change before the render starts if the value is the same
Bailout is for errors; Eager is for speed
Eager updates are only for class components
questions.explanation: Eager updates are an optimization where React skips the whole render pass if it detects the new state is identical to current state using Object.is.

questions.text: In a distributed React system, what is 'Hydration Mismatch' usually a symptom of?
options: Inconsistent data or environment-specific values (e.g., dates/random) between server and client renders
A bug in the React library
questions.explanation: Mismatch occurs when the checksum of the server HTML doesn't match the client's first render, causing a full re-render.

questions.text: What is the 'Compound Component' pattern most helpful for?
options: Creating flexible, highly customizable UI components with shared internal state
questions.explanation: It allows complex components (like Accordions or Tabs) to have a highly readable and declarative structure.

questions.text: What is 'Tree Shaking' specifically looking for in React components?
options: Unused exports and dead code in the ESM import/export tree
questions.explanation: Static analysis by bundlers (like Vite/Webpack) removes code that is never imported or used.

questions.text: What is the 'Prop-in-State' anti-pattern?
options: Passing a state as a prop
Copying a prop into a local state variable, causing 'source of truth' sync issues
questions.explanation: State should be derived from props if possible; mirroring them leads to components that don't update when props change.

questions.text: How does `useTransition` interact with `Suspense`?
options: It prevents a UI from 're-suspending' to a fallback (loading) state when navigating, keeping the current UI visible
It forces the Suspense fallback to show
questions.explanation: Transitions allow for 'seamless' navigation where the old page stays until the new page is ready, rather than showing a spinner.

questions.text: In system design, what is the 'Render-as-Static' (RSC) vs 'Hydrate' cost?
options: RSCs have high CPU cost on client
RSCs have zero client-side bundle/execution cost; Client Components add hydration/JS overhead
questions.explanation: RSCs are the first time React allows 'Zero-Bundle' components, moving the weight to the server and reducing client JS.

questions.text: What is the 'Render Phase' in React?
options: The pure calculation phase where React identifies changes (may be paused/interrupted)
The phase where refs are assigned
questions.explanation: In concurrent React, the render phase is detached from the commit phase to allow for non-blocking work.

questions.text: What is the primary architectural goal of using 'Storybook' in a React project?
options: To provide a sandbox for developing components in isolation and improving consistency
questions.explanation: Storybook facilitates communication between design and engineering by documenting and testing UI states visually.
